title: Reducing standby power in the home
subtitle: Identifying phantom loads and cutting the always-on baseline
excerpt: The average UK home uses 200-450 watts continuously in standby and background loads. Over a year this costs 180-430 pounds at current electricity prices. Most of it can be cut without affecting daily convenience.
sourceNotes: DECC Household Electricity Survey (2012) for baseline standby consumption by appliance category, still the most comprehensive UK domestic standby dataset. Energy Saving Trust standby and always-on guidance (2024 update).

content.text: Every device that remains plugged in draws some power even when not actively being used. The total of all these standby, sleep, and background loads is the always-on baseline: the minimum power the home draws whether anyone is awake or not. In a typical UK home this runs at 200-450 W. At 27p/kWh that is between 1,580 and 3,540 kWh per year, costing 430-950 pounds. The proportion that can be eliminated varies by household, but most homes can cut 100-200 W from the baseline without any practical inconvenience.
What makes up the baseline
Always-on loads (cannot be switched off without losing function)
Refrigerator and freezer: 30-100 W per appliance continuously. This is unavoidable but can be reduced by choosing A-rated appliances and ensuring the rear coils are not in a warm enclosed space.

Router and broadband equipment: 10-30 W. Leaving this on is usually necessary; some routers can be scheduled to power down between midnight and 6 am if the household does not need overnight connectivity.
Security system and smart home hub: 5-20 W. Usually necessary.
Phantom loads (standby draws that serve no necessary function overnight)
Television in standby: 0.5-2 W for modern LED TVs; up to 20 W for older LCD or plasma sets.
Set-top boxes and streaming sticks: 2-15 W in standby. Some set-top boxes update schedules in the background and draw 12-15 W even when the screen is off.
Desktop computers and monitors: 0.5-5 W in sleep; up to 70 W if not in sleep mode.
Microwave and oven with clocks: 2-5 W continuously for the digital clock display.
Phone chargers left plugged in without a phone: 0.1-0.5 W each. Negligible per charger but often five to ten chargers in a household.
Older game consoles in standby: 1-70 W depending on the console generation and sleep mode setting.
How to measure your baseline

A whole-home energy monitor (a clamp on the meter tails connected to an app) shows the baseline directly: look at the reading at 3 am when all active appliances are off. This is the number to reduce. Alternatively, use a plug-in power meter on each appliance to measure its standby draw individually.
Practical reductions
Fit switched multi-way extension leads to entertainment equipment clusters. One switch turns off the TV, set-top box, and games console together. This typically saves 15-30 W overnight.
Enable sleep mode on all computers. A desktop in sleep mode draws under 1 W versus 50-100 W when the screen is on and the machine is idle.
Schedule the router to power down at night if no one needs overnight connectivity. Most routers have a timer setting in the admin interface.
Replace older set-top boxes with a streaming stick when the box reaches end of life. Modern streaming sticks draw 1-2 W in standby versus 12-15 W for an older set-top box.
The numbers

Cutting 100 W from the always-on baseline saves 876 kWh per year at 2025/26 electricity prices of 27p/kWh, that is approximately 237 pounds per year. A 30-pound investment in smart power strips typically pays back in under two months if it eliminates 40 W of phantom load in the living room and office.
